Name: SpectralRoutingForEfficientInference
Title: Spectral Routing for Efficient Neural Network Inference
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spectral properties of hidden state activations can serve as a reliable proxy for determining the optimal computational resources required for efficient inference in neural networks. By dynamically adjusting the precision and computational depth based on the spectral condition number and eigenvalue decay rate of hidden states, we can achieve significant latency reductions while maintaining or even improving accuracy.
Related Work: Recent works such as 'Spectral Quantization Routing' and 'Spectral Latent Steering' have explored the use of spectral properties for optimizing neural network inference. However, these methods often focus on specific aspects like precision allocation or test-time optimization. Our proposal extends these ideas by integrating spectral diagnostics into a comprehensive routing framework that dynamically adjusts both precision and computational depth, providing a more holistic approach to efficient inference.
Abstract: Efficient neural network inference is crucial for deploying models in resource-constrained environments. We propose Spectral Routing, a training-free framework that leverages the spectral properties of hidden state activations to dynamically allocate computational resources. By computing the spectral condition number and eigenvalue decay rate of hidden states, we identify tokens that are robust to low-precision computation and those that require higher precision. Additionally, we use these spectral diagnostics to determine the optimal depth of the network for each input, enabling early exits and reducing unnecessary computations. We theoretically ground our approach using PAC-Bayes bounds and demonstrate its effectiveness on single-GPU setups across various benchmarks. Our method achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic baselines, providing a transparent and reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference.

Experiments.Description: Compute the spectral condition number and eigenvalue decay rate of hidden states for a variety of inputs.
Experiments.Method: Perform a single-pass PCA on the hidden states of initial layers and compute the condition number and eigenvalue decay rate using randomized SVD.
Experiments.Metrics: Spectral condition number, eigenvalue decay rate

Experiments.Description: Evaluate the impact of spectral diagnostics on precision allocation.
Experiments.Method: Dynamically adjust the precision of computations based on the spectral condition number and measure the impact on inference latency and accuracy.

Experiments.Description: Determine the optimal depth of the network for each input using spectral diagnostics.
Experiments.Method: Use the spectral condition number and eigenvalue decay rate to decide when to apply early exits and measure the resulting computational savings.

Experiments.Description: Compare Spectral Routing with static and heuristic baselines.
Experiments.Method: Conduct experiments on single-GPU setups across various benchmarks (e.g., WILDS, CIFAR-10, and COCO) and compare the performance of Spectral Routing with static precision allocation and heuristic methods.
Derive PAC-Bayes bounds that link spectral conditioning to generalization stability under quantization noise and early exits.

Risk Factors and Limitations: The computational overhead of spectral diagnostics may introduce additional latency, particularly in real-time applications.
The effectiveness of spectral diagnostics may vary across different neural network architectures and datasets.
The theoretical bounds derived may not always hold in practice, especially under extreme distribution shifts.
